title: Neon vs Supabase
description: A technical comparison of Neon and Supabase for hosted Postgres workloads in 2026. Covers architecture, branching, autoscaling, connection pooling, replication, pricing, and the trade-offs between a serverless Postgres engine and a full backend-as-a-service platform.
introduction:
If you want managed Postgres without standing up RDS yourself, Neon and Supabase are the two names that come up first. They look superficially similar — both run Postgres, both have generous free tiers, both ship branching, both index pgvector for AI workloads, and as of 2026 both ship a managed authentication layer. Underneath, they are very different products solving very different problems.

Neon is a serverless Postgres engine. The team rebuilt the bottom half of Postgres in Rust so that compute and storage can scale independently, then wrapped it in a control plane that exposes branching, autoscaling, and scale-to-zero as first-class primitives. It looks like Postgres to your application — same wire protocol, same extensions, same SQL — but every dial that used to require operations work (provisioning, vacuuming pages off hot disks, building read replicas, restoring from a snapshot) is now a metadata operation. Databricks acquired Neon in 2025 and used the headroom to drop storage prices from $1.75 to $0.35 per GB-month and double the free tier's compute allowance. Neon Auth (built on Better Auth) reached general availability in late 2025, closing the one application-layer gap reviewers used to flag, and the same Neon storage engine now powers Databricks' Lakebase as a technical proof point. The 2026 cadence has been heavy on AI-agent tooling — Neon Launchpad provisions a new database in under 350 ms, and the CLI ships init flows for Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Antigravity, Cline, and most other AI assistants in the wild.

Supabase is a backend-as-a-service built around a regular Postgres instance. Each project gets a dedicated Postgres VM plus a stack of services on top: PostgREST for an instant REST API, GoTrue for authentication, a Realtime server for websocket subscriptions, an S3-compatible Storage gateway, Supavisor for connection pooling, and Edge Functions on Deno for serverless logic. The marketing line is "open-source Firebase alternative," and that framing is accurate — you trade some database-level control for a batteries-included backend that can ship a working product in a weekend.

The choice is rarely "Postgres vs Postgres." It's about how much of the surrounding stack you want bundled with the database. This comparison walks through the dimensions that actually matter when picking between them: how branching works at the storage layer, what scale-to-zero means for your bill, where the connection pooling lives, and which platform handles the long tail of database operations (PITR, replicas, version coverage) without forcing you off the happy path.

toolA.name: Neon
toolA.description: A serverless Postgres platform with separated compute and storage. Branching is copy-on-write at the storage layer, autoscaling is built in, and idle databases scale to zero. Postgres-native, so existing extensions, ORMs, and tooling work without modification.
pros: Copy-on-write branching at the storage layer creates a full data branch in milliseconds regardless of database size
Scale-to-zero on idle and autoscaling between 0.25 and 16 CU mean you only pay for the compute you actually use
Custom Rust storage engine (Pageservers + Safekeepers) decouples compute restarts from data durability and enables sub-second PITR at any LSN
Postgres 14, 15, 16, 17 stable plus 18 in preview — the broadest version coverage among managed serverless Postgres providers
Native serverless driver over WebSockets removes TCP setup cost on Vercel Functions, Cloudflare Workers, and other edge runtimes
Read replicas are first-class compute endpoints that share storage with the primary, so adding one is an API call rather than a streaming-replication setup
Postgres-native: every wire-protocol client, ORM, migration tool, and extension that runs on vanilla Postgres works without special drivers
Neon Auth (built on Better Auth) ships managed authentication with users in the neon_auth schema, isolated per branch, and queryable via SQL and RLS
cons: No bundled object storage, realtime server, or edge-functions runtime — pair Neon with S3/R2 and your own serverless platform when you need them
Cold-start latency on scale-to-zero compute is typically 300-500 ms, which matters for the very first request after an idle window
Compute sizes above 16 CU are fixed-size (no autoscaling), so the largest workloads still need manual capacity planning
Some Postgres extensions take longer to land than on a self-managed instance because they have to be vetted against the Pageserver
Smaller community than Supabase for application-layer recipes, since Neon is squarely a database product

toolB.name: Supabase
toolB.description: An open-source backend-as-a-service built around a dedicated Postgres instance. Bundles PostgREST APIs, GoTrue authentication, a Realtime server, S3-compatible storage, Supavisor pooling, and Deno-based Edge Functions in one platform.
pros: Bundles authentication, REST/GraphQL, realtime subscriptions, storage, and edge functions so a small team can ship a backend without integrating multiple vendors
Row Level Security policies move authorization into the database, which is genuinely powerful when designed for from day one
Supavisor (Elixir-based pooler) handles tens of thousands of concurrent serverless connections per project
Generated TypeScript types from the live schema make end-to-end type safety nearly free
Local development environment runs the full stack in Docker so you can prototype offline
Vibrant community of templates and starter apps for common product shapes (B2B SaaS, marketplaces, mobile apps)
Active platform iteration: PostgREST v14 added ~20% RPS on GET requests, and Supabase Warehouse (built on pg_duckdb) is on the public roadmap for analytics workloads
cons: Branching creates a brand-new Postgres instance and replays migrations — slower and storage-heavier than copy-on-write, and starts with empty data unless you supply a seed script
Free-tier projects auto-pause after 7 days of inactivity, which makes the free plan unsuitable for low-traffic production workloads
Each project is a dedicated Postgres VM, so compute cannot scale to zero and idle projects keep accruing the base compute charge
Pro plan adds metered MAU pricing on top of compute — at $0.00325 per MAU above the included 100K, an auth-heavy product can quickly outrun the headline $25/month figure
More moving parts in the stack (Realtime, Auth, Storage, PostgREST) means more services to reason about during incidents — the trade-off for getting them out of the box

toolA.value: Disaggregated Postgres: stateless compute talks to a custom Rust storage engine (Pageservers + Safekeepers) over the network

toolB.value: Dedicated single-VM Postgres per project with bundled service sidecars (Auth, Realtime, Storage, Edge Functions)

toolA.value: Copy-on-write at the storage layer; branches are O(1) metadata operations and include the full parent dataset

toolB.value: Migration-based; provisions a new Postgres instance, replays migrations from Git, and starts data-less unless seeded

toolA.value: Compute autoscales 0.25-16 CU on demand; databases scale to zero when idle and resume on first connection

toolB.value: Fixed compute sizes per plan (Micro/Small/Medium/etc.); no scale-to-zero, paid plans charge for idle compute

toolA.value: Postgres 14, 15, 16, 17 stable plus 18 in preview; you choose the major version per project

toolB.value: Tracks one current Postgres major (15+) for new projects; older versions on existing projects until forced upgrade

toolA.value: PgBouncer endpoint on every branch plus a native serverless driver over WebSockets for edge runtimes

toolB.value: Supavisor (Elixir) multi-tenant pooler handling tens of thousands of concurrent connections per project

toolA.value: Read replicas share storage with the primary; adding one is a compute-only operation with no replication lag setup

toolB.value: Read replicas available on Pro and above; provisioned as separate Postgres instances with streaming replication

toolA.value: Sub-second LSN-level PITR on any branch; up to 30 days of restore window on Scale, 7 days on Launch

toolB.value: Daily automated backups on Pro; PITR (1-day window) and longer retention on Team and above

toolA.value: pgvector with HNSW indexes; autoscaling makes RAG workloads cheap during sparse traffic and capable under bursts

toolB.value: pgvector with HNSW; widely used and well-documented for embedding storage alongside application data

toolA.value: Neon Auth (built on Better Auth) stores users/sessions/config in the neon_auth schema; queryable with SQL and RLS; per-branch auth environments mirror database branching

toolB.value: GoTrue auth with email, OAuth, magic links, phone, SSO; the most mature managed-Postgres auth offering with deep RLS integration

toolA.value: Native Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY plus logical replication; subscribers integrate via your own WebSocket layer

toolB.value: Realtime server multiplexes Postgres changes onto WebSocket channels; 10K+ concurrent subscribers per project

toolA.value: No bundled object storage or function runtime; pairs with S3, R2, or any serverless platform of your choice

toolB.value: S3-compatible Storage with image transformations and Edge Functions on Deno included in every plan

toolA.value: Usage-based with $5/month minimum on Launch; compute $0.14-$0.26 per CU-hour, storage $0.35/GB-month with scale-to-zero

toolB.value: $25/month per project on Pro plus metered overage (MAU, egress, compute); idle projects still accrue the base charge

toolA.value: Built-in Monitoring tab with CPU, memory, connections, and query history; metrics exported to Datadog and OpenTelemetry on paid plans

toolB.value: Reports dashboard with database, auth, storage, and edge-function usage; query insights via the integrated Postgres advisor

toolA.value: Postgres-native: any tool that talks to vanilla Postgres works (Atlas, Drizzle Kit, Prisma migrate, Sqitch, Flyway, etc.)

toolB.value: Tied to the Supabase CLI's migration workflow with diffs against local containers; works well with Git but is a learn-this-tool path

toolA.value: Apache 2.0; full storage engine + control plane on GitHub, single-Docker NeonD path, Kubernetes operator for production self-hosting

toolB.value: Apache 2.0 across every component; the most polished self-host story in this space with mature docker-compose templates

toolA.value: No bundled API layer; pair with PostgREST, Hasura, or pg_graphql yourself when you need an instant REST or GraphQL endpoint

toolB.value: PostgREST and pg_graphql both ship enabled, with auto-generated REST and GraphQL APIs that mirror the schema and respect RLS

toolA.value: Neon Launchpad provisions a database in under 350 ms; claimable-postgres MCP skill lets agents grab a connection string autonomously; CLI init supports Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Cline, Goose, Antigravity, and more

toolB.value: MCP server for Supabase exposes schema and RLS context; `mcp-use` SDK runs MCP servers on Edge Functions; CLI integrates with the major AI IDEs but the agent-native workflow story is younger

useCases.scenario: Vercel or Cloudflare Workers app that needs Postgres with low cold-start latency from the edge
useCases.explanation: Neon's WebSocket-based serverless driver was designed for this workload. It keeps compute warm during traffic, scales to zero between bursts, and the native driver avoids the per-request TCP/TLS setup that dominates latency from edge runtimes. Pricing tracks actual usage rather than a fixed per-project charge, which compounds across preview deployments.

useCases.scenario: CI pipeline that wants a fresh, fully-populated database per pull request and ephemeral preview environment
useCases.explanation: Branching is the load-bearing feature here. Neon's copy-on-write semantics create a writable branch with the parent's full dataset in under a second regardless of size, run the test or preview against it, then garbage-collect the diverged pages when the PR closes. Migration-based branching has to wait for migrations to replay and starts from an empty schema, which makes integration tests against production-like data harder.

useCases.scenario: B2C consumer product that needs auth, storage, file uploads, and realtime presence on day one
useCases.explanation: Supabase's bundled stack genuinely shortens time-to-launch when you'd otherwise be wiring up Auth0, S3, Pusher, and PostgREST yourself. The trade-off is operational coupling — if Realtime has an incident, your database can be affected — but for a small team shipping a v1, the integration cost saved is usually worth it.

useCases.scenario: Background-worker product with bursty Sidekiq/BullMQ traffic and long idle stretches overnight
useCases.explanation: Scale-to-zero is the only mechanism that makes these workloads cheap. A 2-CU Neon database that handles a daily burst and idles the rest of the time costs a fraction of a Supabase Pro project running a Small instance 24/7, even before counting storage savings.

useCases.scenario: Mobile app with thousands of simultaneous WebSocket connections subscribing to row changes
useCases.explanation: Supabase Realtime is purpose-built for this and ships with the platform. You can build the same thing on Neon with logical replication and your own server, but the integration cost is real, especially for the auth-aware row-level filtering that Realtime gives you for free against RLS policies.

useCases.scenario: RAG pipeline storing tens of millions of embeddings with sparse, spiky retrieval traffic
useCases.explanation: pgvector works well on both. Neon wins on cost because the compute scales down when retrieval is idle, then ramps to 16 CU when a batch indexing job kicks off. On a fixed-size Supabase instance you provision for the spike and pay for it all month.

useCases.scenario: AI agent or autonomous tool that needs to spin up disposable test databases with specific seed data
useCases.explanation: Neon ships specifically for this loop. Neon Launchpad provisions a brand-new database in under 350 ms, the claimable-postgres MCP skill lets an assistant claim one autonomously, and `npx neonctl init` integrates with Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Antigravity, Cline, Goose, and most other AI assistants. Branching from a known-good parent database in milliseconds is the same primitive used to reset state between runs. Migration-based provisioning is too slow for an agent loop.

useCases.scenario: Existing app with a complex schema and hundreds of migrations moving from RDS to a managed serverless option
useCases.explanation: Postgres-native compatibility matters most here. Neon supports Postgres 14-18 and accepts the same migrations, extensions, and ORM configurations as RDS. Supabase imposes a coupled Auth/Realtime/Storage stack that may conflict with your existing application-layer choices.

useCases.scenario: Internal admin tool or back-office app that wants instant REST and GraphQL APIs over a schema
useCases.explanation: PostgREST and pg_graphql come for free with Supabase, and the auto-generated TypeScript types make a typed admin UI nearly drop-in. On Neon you'd reach for Hasura, PostgREST, or write the API yourself, which is fine but adds a moving piece you don't strictly need.

useCases.scenario: Mobile-first product where the iOS, Android, and React Native SDK story matters as much as the database
useCases.explanation: Supabase ships well-tested mobile SDKs that bundle auth, realtime, and storage in one client. Neon is a database — perfectly usable from a mobile app via your own backend, but you'll be doing the SDK integration work yourself.

faqs.question: Is Neon really just Postgres, or are there compatibility caveats?
faqs.answer: It is real Postgres at the wire-protocol level — same SQL, same JDBC/psycopg/pgx drivers, same migration tools, same ORMs. The compatibility caveats are around extensions: a small number of extensions that depend on writing to local disk (file_fdw, certain background workers) are not available because Neon's storage is remote. The mainstream extensions (pgvector, PostGIS, pg_stat_statements, pgcrypto, etc.) all work.

faqs.question: Doesn't scale-to-zero mean unacceptable cold starts?
faqs.answer: Cold starts on Neon are typically 300-500 ms because the compute layer is stateless and only needs to attach to the existing storage. For most B2B SaaS and developer-facing products, that's invisible behind a normal page load. For latency-critical user-facing endpoints, you can either disable scale-to-zero on the production branch (paid plans) or keep a synthetic ping warming the endpoint.

faqs.question: If I'm already on Supabase, is migrating to Neon hard?
faqs.answer: The database side is straightforward: pg_dump from Supabase, pg_restore into Neon, repoint your connection string. The harder part is the auth/storage/realtime layers, which are Supabase-specific. You either keep Supabase for those and use Neon as the application database, or you replace each layer (Clerk/WorkOS for auth, R2/S3 for storage, your own WebSocket gateway for realtime) at your own pace.

faqs.question: Does Supabase support branching the same way Neon does?
faqs.answer: Supabase introduced branching, but the implementation is fundamentally different. A Supabase branch is a brand-new Postgres instance with the schema replayed from your migrations directory, optionally seeded by a script you provide. Useful for staging and CI on schema changes, but it isn't a copy of production data and provisioning is closer to seconds-to-minutes than milliseconds. Neon's branches share storage pages with the parent and only diverge on writes.

faqs.question: What about Postgres extensions for AI workloads — pgvector, pgrag, pg_search?
faqs.answer: Both platforms support pgvector with HNSW indexing. Neon additionally ships pgrag (Neon's own RAG-pipeline extension) and is typically faster to add new versions of pgvector because the storage engine isolation makes upgrades safer. Supabase's pgvector support is mature and well-documented for application-layer integrations through its SDKs.

faqs.question: How do the free tiers actually compare in practice?
faqs.answer: Neon's free tier gives you 100 CU-hours per month, 0.5 GB storage per branch, up to 100 projects, and never auto-pauses idle databases. Supabase's free tier gives you 500 MB database, 1 GB storage, 50K MAUs, 500K edge function calls, but pauses any project after 7 days of inactivity. For a side project that gets traffic in fits and starts, Neon's behavior is more forgiving — you don't have to remember to ping it.

faqs.question: Which one has better tooling for AI agents and Cursor/Claude Code workflows?
faqs.answer: Neon publishes an MCP server and a clean management API, and the branching model maps naturally to agent reset-state semantics: spawn a branch, run experiment, drop branch. Supabase's CLI is excellent for human workflows but the schema/migration model assumes a developer-driven loop rather than ephemeral agent-driven environments.

faqs.question: Doesn't Supabase have an auth advantage that Neon can't match?
faqs.answer: Less true now than it used to be. Neon Auth (built on Better Auth) reached general availability in late 2025: users, sessions, and configuration land in the neon_auth schema and are queryable like any other Postgres data, RLS policies work out of the box, and every database branch gets its own isolated auth environment. Supabase's GoTrue is still the more mature product — broader provider list, more SSO integrations, longer-running ecosystem — but the binary 'Neon doesn't do auth' framing no longer holds.

decisionMatrix.criteria: You want a Postgres database that scales to zero when idle and bills only for actual usage
You need authentication, storage, realtime, and edge functions bundled with the database
You run preview environments per pull request with full production-like data
You're building a product that lives or dies on row-level WebSocket subscriptions
Your workload is bursty with long idle stretches (background workers, weekly batch jobs, RAG pipelines)
You want the broadest Postgres major version coverage and to choose your version per project
You want to ship a working backend in a weekend without picking auth, storage, and realtime providers
You're migrating from RDS or self-managed Postgres and want minimal application changes

verdict.summary: Neon and Supabase solve different problems and both do their thing well. Neon is a serverless Postgres engine — branching, autoscaling, and scale-to-zero are baked into the storage layer, and the wire-protocol contract is plain Postgres. Supabase is a backend-as-a-service built around a regular Postgres instance, bundling auth, realtime, storage, and edge functions so a small team can ship an entire product without picking each layer separately. The choice is rarely about which is better in the abstract — it's about whether you want a database that fits anywhere or a backend that ships faster.
verdict.recommendation: Choose Neon when Postgres is the primary thing you need from the platform: serverless apps, ephemeral CI environments, agent-driven test loops, RAG workloads with bursty traffic, or migrations away from RDS where you don't want to inherit a coupled BaaS stack. Choose Supabase when the auth/storage/realtime/auto-API bundle is the real value proposition — internal tools, mobile-first products, and v1 launches where saving on integration time matters more than fine-grained control over each layer.
